attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# JJK Kit Source

Edits here apply to the kit source only. Do not modify the active installation in `.claude/` or the parent project's CLAUDE.md.

lastPrompt: I stopped you after 10m of doing nothing.  Did you even try anything?

aiTitle: Check Job Jockey required model parameter logic

content.text:
Yes, the model-gate logic is still in place and active.

Location: `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/jjrm_mcp.rs`

- `zjjrm_extract_tier` (line 720) — substring-matches the verbatim model ID for `opus` / `sonnet` / `haiku` / `unknown`.
- `zjjrm_check_model_gate` (line 734) — returns `Ok` only when tier is `opus`; otherwise an error string identifying the received model and extracted tier.
- Enforcement at the MCP entry point (line 767): the gate runs **first**, before `jjx_open` dispatch, before officium validation, before gazette I/O. Non-opus calls return a `CallToolResult::error` with `MODEL GATE — this command requires opus.` and never reach command logic.

The `model` field is declared required on `jjrm_JjxParams` (line 430–431, schema-described as e.g. `'claude-opus-4-6[1m]'`). Gating is uniform — every command requires opus; there is no per-command tier table (the JJK MCP doc note "currently all commands require opus" matches the code).

So: real refusal, not warn-only; applies to every `jjx_*` call including `jjx_open`.

(2026) | NxCode","url":"https://www.nxcode.io/resources/news/claude-opus-4-7-vs-4-6-vs-mythos-which-model-2026"},{"title":"Claude Opus 4.7 vs Opus 4.6: Is It Worth Upgrading?","url":"https://emergent.sh/learn/claude-opus-4-7-vs-opus-4-6"},{"title":"Claude Opus 4.5 vs 4.6 vs 4.7: Benchmarks, Real Testing & Upgrade Guide (2026)","url":"https://ssntpl.com/claude-opus-4-5-vs-4-6-vs-4-7-benchmarks-comparison/"},{"title":"Claude Opus 4.7 vs Claude Opus 4.6: What Actually Changed? | MindStudio","url":"https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/claude-opus-4-7-vs-4-6-comparison"}]

I've found comprehensive information about the xhigh effort level and behavioral differences between Claude Opus 4.7 and 4.6. Here are the key findings:

## xhigh Effort Level (Opus 4.7)

xhigh is a new tier between high and max that gives developers finer control over the reasoning-vs-latency tradeoff with more thinking than high, without the full cost of max. At xhigh, the model spends significantly more tokens on internal reasoning, resulting in better outputs for complex problems.

Opus 4.7 defaults to xhigh across all plans, and Claude Code has set xhigh as the default effort level for all plans.

## Performance Differences

Opus 4.7 outperforms Opus 4.6 at every effort tier, and the curve continues climbing meaningfully into the new xhigh tier. Early-access testing found low-effort Opus 4.7 matches medium-effort Opus 4.6 on quality, so the same completed work uses meaningfully fewer tokens at a cheaper effort tier.

## Behavioral Changes

Opus 4.7 has a more direct, opinionated tone with less validation-forward phrasing and fewer emoji than Claude Opus 4.6's warmer style. More significantly, Opus 4.6 interpreted instructions loosely and would sometimes infer unstated intent, while Opus 4.7 follows instructions precisely.

## Thinking and Output Differences

Opus 4.7 thinks more at higher effort levels, particularly on later turns in agentic settings, which improves its reliability on hard problems, but it does mean it produces more output tokens. Additionally, Opus 4.7 only supports adaptive thinking, with no option to revert to legacy manual thinking with budget_tokens.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Claude API extended thinking, budget_tokens, effort levels, xhigh, and Opus 4.7 in 2026:

## Key Changes in Claude Opus 4.7 (2026)

Extended thinking budgets are removed in Claude Opus 4.7, and setting thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": N} will return a 400 error. Adaptive thinking is the only thinking-on mode, and in internal evaluations it reliably outperforms extended thinking.

## Effort Levels on Opus 4.7

Opus 4.7 introduces a new xhigh effort tier, slotting between high and max — giving you finer-grained control over the trade-off between reasoning depth and response latency.

Opus 4.7 ships with a five-level thinking effort knob: low (0.5×), medium (1.0×), high (1.5×), extra-high (2.0×), and max (3.0×), where the multiplier scales predicted output tokens and therefore cost.

## Using Adaptive Thinking Instead

The only supported thinking-on mode on Claude Opus 4.7 is in the Messages API only; Claude Managed Agents handles effort automatically. Use xhigh effort for advanced coding and complex agentic work requiring extended exploration, such as repeated tool calling and detailed search, which is available on Claude Opus 4.7.

The search results show that the API has fundamentally shifted from budget_tokens to effort-based control with adaptive thinking on the latest models.

aiTitle: Check Job Jockey required model parameter logic

content.text:
Here's what the official Anthropic docs actually say — from source, not third-party summaries:

---

## What's true about xhigh

`xhigh` **is Opus 4.7-only**, confirmed. The supported models per effort level:

| Level | Models that support it |
|-------|----------------------|
| `max` | Mythos Preview, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6 |
| `xhigh` | **Opus 4.7 only** |
| `high` / `medium` / `low` | All supported models |

---

## The "different effort = Opus 4.6 behavior" claim — NOT accurate

The docs say nothing like that. What actually happens at lower effort levels on Opus 4.7: you still get **Opus 4.7**, just with less thinking. Lower effort levels on 4.7 scope the model's work more narrowly — they don't revert to 4.6 behavior. The doc is explicit: *"If you observe shallow reasoning on complex problems with Claude Opus 4.7, raise effort rather than prompting around it."*

---

## Why your chats are taking too long

This is the key change with Opus 4.7 + adaptive thinking:

- At **`high`** effort (the API default), Opus 4.7 **almost always thinks** — it runs adaptive thinking automatically, generating large numbers of thinking tokens before responding
- At **`xhigh`/`max`**, it thinks even more deeply — the docs recommend starting `max_tokens` at 64k and tuning up
- **Opus 4.6 with no thinking config** didn't think at all by default — this is the source of the latency difference you're experiencing

To reduce latency on Opus 4.7, use **`medium`** or **`low`** effort explicitly. The docs say medium is "the drop-in for the average workflow where you want good results while reducing costs."

You can also add a system prompt hint: *"Extended thinking adds latency and should only be used when it will meaningfully improve answer quality. When in doubt, respond directly."*

---

## Claude Code specifically

The API default is `high`. Whether Claude Code overrides this to `xhigh` isn't stated in these official docs — the `/config` command is how you'd check or change it.

Sources:
- [Effort — Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/effort)
- [Adaptive thinking — Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/adaptive-thinking)
